title_en: The Attribute of Seeing and Hearing, and Texts About the Divine Face and Hand
title_ar: صفتا السمع والبصر وما ورد من الوجه واليدين
content_en:
Al-Bayhaqi devotes careful attention to the texts in the Quran and Sunnah that attribute to Allah modalities of perception — hearing (sam') and sight (basar) — and the more controversial 'anthropomorphic' expressions that speak of Allah's face (wajh), hands (yad/yadayn), eyes ('ayn), and shin (saq). His treatment of these texts exemplifies the Ash'ari methodology of affirming the attributes while interpreting those expressions that might imply physical form in a manner consistent with divine transcendence.

The attributes of hearing and sight are among those most clearly and repeatedly affirmed in the Quran. Allah is described as 'al-Sami' al-Basir' (the All-Hearing, All-Seeing) in numerous verses. These attributes are 'affirmed' (ithbat) in the fullest sense: they are real divine attributes, not merely metaphors. Allah truly hears all sounds and sees all visible things, in a manner befitting His majesty. The divine hearing and sight are distinguished from their human counterparts in the same ways that all divine attributes differ from created ones: they are unlimited, eternal, not dependent on physical organs, and encompassing of everything in existence.

The texts that speak of Allah's 'face' (wajh) are interpreted by al-Bayhaqi and the Ash'ari school as referring to the divine essence itself, or in some cases to Allah's 'direction' (jiha) in the context of worship. When the Quran says 'Everything will perish except His face' (28:88), the Ash'ari interpretation is that this means 'everything will perish except His essence' — the face being used as a metonym for the whole being, as is common in classical Arabic. Similarly, 'Wherever you turn, there is the face of Allah' (2:115) is interpreted as affirming the omnipresence of Allah's knowledge and power, not a literal divine face in every direction.

The texts about the divine 'hand' (yad) or 'two hands' (yadayn) are among the most discussed in the tradition. The Quran says: 'The Jews said: "Allah's hand is chained." Nay, both His hands are extended' (5:64). And: 'What prevented you from prostrating before what I created with My two hands?' (38:75). Al-Bayhaqi presents two major interpretive approaches. The first, associated with the path of tafwid (committing the meaning to Allah), affirms that Allah has attributes He calls 'hands' whose nature is known only to Him, while firmly denying any physical form. The second, the path of ta'wil, interprets 'hand' as signifying power (qudra) or grace (ni'ma), both of which are meanings 'yad' carries in classical Arabic.

Al-Bayhaqi cites the positions of the early scholars on these matters with care. He shows that the Companions and Successors generally understood these texts in a way that preserved divine transcendence. He quotes Imam al-Bayhaqi's teacher, Imam Abu Bakr ibn Furak, and ultimately traces the methodology back to Imam al-Ash'ari himself and to Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal — demonstrating that the Ash'ari approach is continuous with the best of the earlier tradition, not an innovation. The goal is not to empty the texts of meaning but to protect them from the distortion of anthropomorphism (tashbih) while preserving their affirmation of real divine attributes.

The practical significance of this theological discussion is considerable. The believer who understands that Allah truly hears and sees — that nothing escapes His awareness — will be more sincere in worship, more careful in conduct, and more conscious of the divine presence in every moment of life. The Quran's use of expressions like 'face' and 'hand' is not a concession to human limitation but an invitation to a relationship with Allah that is personal, responsive, and real. Allah is not an abstract philosophical principle but the living God who hears prayers, sees the condition of His servants, and responds to sincere turning toward Him. Al-Bayhaqi's careful theological work ultimately serves this pastoral purpose: ensuring that Muslims understand their Lord in a way that is both intellectually sound and spiritually nourishing.
content_ar: السمع والبصر صفتان ثابتتان لله تعالى بالقرآن والسنة، وما ورد من الوجه واليدين يُثبَت لله على الوجه اللائق بجلاله من غير تأويل يُفضي إلى التعطيل.
